section.title: The wedge product
section.p: ended with covectors. A covector measures one tangent vector. But a surface patch gives two tangent vectors, and a tiny box gives three. To measure oriented area and oriented volume, we need a way to combine covectors.
That way is the wedge product.

subsection.title: Oriented area from two covectors
subsection.p: Start with a small parallelogram in the plane. Its first edge is \mathbf u=(3,1), and its second edge is \mathbf v=(1,4). The ordered pair (\mathbf u,\mathbf v) means that we travel first in the \mathbf u-direction and then in the \mathbf v-direction. That order gives the parallelogram an orientation.

figure.caption: The signed area of a parallelogram depends on the order of its two edge vectors.

subsection.p:
The signed area is the determinant
\begin{vmatrix} 3\amp1\\ 1\amp4 \end{vmatrix} = 3\cdot4-1\cdot1 = 11.
Now write the same computation using the coordinate covectors dx,\qquad dy. They read components:
dx(\mathbf u)=3, \qquad dy(\mathbf u)=1,
and
dx(\mathbf v)=1, \qquad dy(\mathbf v)=4.
The signed area is dx(\mathbf u)dy(\mathbf v)-dx(\mathbf v)dy(\mathbf u). For our two vectors, this gives 3\cdot4-1\cdot1=11.
The symbol dx\wedge dy is the basic oriented area form in the xy-plane. It eats two tangent vectors and returns their signed xy-area.

example.title: The same parallelogram with reversed order

statement.p:
Use the same vectors
\mathbf u=(3,1), \qquad \mathbf v=(1,4),
but feed them to dx\wedge dy in the opposite order:
(dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf v,\mathbf u) \amp= dx(\mathbf v)dy(\mathbf u)-dx(\mathbf u)dy(\mathbf v) \amp= 1\cdot1-3\cdot4 \amp= -11.
The geometric parallelogram is the same size, but its orientation has been reversed.

subsection.p: This example contains the whole idea. The wedge product is built to measure oriented parallelograms.

statement.p:
Let \alpha and \beta be covectors at a point. Their wedge product \alpha\wedge\beta is the rule that takes an ordered pair of tangent vectors (\mathbf u,\mathbf v) and returns
\boxed{ (\alpha\wedge\beta)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) = \alpha(\mathbf u)\beta(\mathbf v) - \alpha(\mathbf v)\beta(\mathbf u). }

subsection.p: The two terms are the two possible ways to match the covectors with the two vectors. The minus sign is what remembers orientation.

example.title: A wedge product that measures a slanted coordinate area

statement.p:
Let
\alpha=2\,dx+dy, \qquad \beta=dx+3\,dy.
Use the vectors
\mathbf u=(1,2), \qquad \mathbf v=(4,-1).
First evaluate the covectors:
\alpha(\mathbf u)=2(1)+2=4, \qquad \alpha(\mathbf v)=2(4)-1=7,
and
\beta(\mathbf u)=1+3(2)=7, \qquad \beta(\mathbf v)=4+3(-1)=1.
Therefore
(\alpha\wedge\beta)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= \alpha(\mathbf u)\beta(\mathbf v) - \alpha(\mathbf v)\beta(\mathbf u) \amp= 4\cdot1-7\cdot7 \amp= -45.
The negative sign says that the ordered measurements \alpha,\beta see the ordered vectors \mathbf u,\mathbf v with the opposite orientation.

subsection.p: A 1-form measures one vector. A wedge of two 1-forms measures two vectors at once. That is why it becomes a 2-form.

subsection.title: Antisymmetry: dx\wedge dy=-dy\wedge dx
subsection.p:
The wedge product changes sign when its factors are swapped. Check this directly:
(\beta\wedge\alpha)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= \beta(\mathbf u)\alpha(\mathbf v) - \beta(\mathbf v)\alpha(\mathbf u) \amp= -\left[ \alpha(\mathbf u)\beta(\mathbf v) - \alpha(\mathbf v)\beta(\mathbf u) \right].
So
\boxed{ \beta\wedge\alpha=-\alpha\wedge\beta. }

example.title: The same area measured with dy\wedge dx

statement.p:
Return again to
\mathbf u=(3,1), \qquad \mathbf v=(1,4).
Then
(dy\wedge dx)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= dy(\mathbf u)dx(\mathbf v)-dy(\mathbf v)dx(\mathbf u) \amp= 1\cdot1-4\cdot3 \amp= -11.
But (dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v)=11. So dy\wedge dx measures the same area with the opposite orientation.

subsection.p: This is the first algebra rule of wedge products:
That sign is not a bookkeeping trick. It is the algebraic form of a geometric fact: swapping the two edge directions of a parallelogram reverses its orientation.

subsection.title: Why dx\wedge dx=0
subsection.p: The geometry is simple. A single coordinate reading repeated twice cannot measure area. Area needs two independent directions of measurement. Measuring the x-component twice collapses the picture onto one line.

example.title: Parallel edge vectors give zero area

statement.p:
Let
\mathbf u=(2,1), \qquad \mathbf v=(6,3).
The second vector is \mathbf v=3\mathbf u. The parallelogram has collapsed to a line segment, so its signed area should be 0. Indeed,
(dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= 2\cdot3-6\cdot1 \amp= 0.
The wedge product automatically detects the collapse.

subsection.p: This is one reason wedge notation is so useful. The rules dx\wedge dx=0 and dx\wedge dy=-dy\wedge dx make repeated or reversed directions behave exactly as oriented area says they should.

subsection.title: Building 2-forms and 3-forms
subsection.p: In the plane, there is only one basic oriented area form: dx\wedge dy. So every 2-form in the plane has the shape M(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy.

In space, a small surface patch can cast shadows on three coordinate planes. That is why three basic 2-forms appear: dy\wedge dz,\qquad dz\wedge dx,\qquad dx\wedge dy. Each one measures a signed shadow.

example.title: A 2-form measuring a tilted surface patch

statement.p:
Suppose a small surface patch has tangent edge vectors
\mathbf u=(1,2,0), \qquad \mathbf v=(0,1,3).
Compute the three coordinate shadow areas:
(dy\wedge dz)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= 2\cdot3-1\cdot0 = 6, (dz\wedge dx)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= 0\cdot0-3\cdot1 = -3, (dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= 1\cdot1-0\cdot2 = 1.
Now let \Phi=2\,dy\wedge dz-dz\wedge dx+4\,dx\wedge dy. Then
\Phi(\mathbf u,\mathbf v) \amp= 2(6)-(-3)+4(1) \amp= 19.
The form did not measure ordinary surface area. It measured an oriented combination of coordinate-plane shadows.

subsection.p: A 3-form measures an oriented box. The basic one in space is dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. It eats three tangent vectors and returns the signed volume of the parallelepiped they span.

statement.p:
Let
\mathbf u=(1,0,0), \qquad \mathbf v=(1,2,0), \qquad \mathbf w=(0,1,3).
Then
(dx\wedge dy\wedge dz)(\mathbf u,\mathbf v,\mathbf w) \amp= \det \begin{pmatrix} 1\amp1\amp0\\ 0\amp2\amp1\\ 0\amp0\amp3 \end{pmatrix} \amp= 6.
So the oriented volume of the box spanned by these three ordered vectors is 6. If we swap \mathbf u and \mathbf v, the value becomes -6.

subsection.title: Determinants as wedge-product coefficients
subsection.p: The wedge product explains why determinants keep appearing in multivariable calculus. They are the coefficients of oriented area and oriented volume.

Start in the plane. Let
\alpha=a\,dx+b\,dy, \qquad \beta=c\,dx+d\,dy.
Then
\alpha\wedge\beta \amp= (a\,dx+b\,dy)\wedge(c\,dx+d\,dy) \amp= ac\,dx\wedge dx +ad\,dx\wedge dy +bc\,dy\wedge dx +bd\,dy\wedge dy.
Now use
dx\wedge dx=0, \qquad dy\wedge dy=0,
and dy\wedge dx=-dx\wedge dy. The result is
\boxed{ \alpha\wedge\beta=(ad-bc)\,dx\wedge dy. }
The coefficient ad-bc is a 2\times2 determinant:
\boxed{ ad-bc = \begin{vmatrix} a\amp b\\ c\amp d \end{vmatrix}. }

example.title: The determinant inside a wedge product

statement.p:
Let
\alpha=2\,dx+dy, \qquad \beta=dx+4\,dy.
Then
\alpha\wedge\beta \amp= (2\cdot4-1\cdot1)\,dx\wedge dy \amp= 7\,dx\wedge dy.
The number 7 is the signed area scale factor hidden in the two covectors.

subsection.p:
In space, wedge two 1-forms: \alpha=a\,dx+b\,dy+c\,dz, and \beta=p\,dx+q\,dy+r\,dz. A direct expansion gives
\boxed{ \alpha\wedge\beta = (br-cq)\,dy\wedge dz + (cp-ar)\,dz\wedge dx + (aq-bp)\,dx\wedge dy. }
The three coefficients are the components of the cross product of the coefficient vectors:
(a,b,c)\times(p,q,r) = (br-cq,\ cp-ar,\ aq-bp).
So the cross product is also hiding inside the wedge product. The wedge product keeps the result as a 2-form, while the cross product turns the same coefficients into a vector.

For three 1-forms, \alpha=a\,dx+b\,dy+c\,dz, \beta=p\,dx+q\,dy+r\,dz, and \gamma=m\,dx+n\,dy+s\,dz, the wedge product is
\boxed{ \alpha\wedge\beta\wedge\gamma = \begin{vmatrix} a\amp b\amp c\\ p\amp q\amp r\\ m\amp n\amp s \end{vmatrix} dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. }

statement.p:
Let
\alpha=dx+dy, \qquad \beta=2\,dy+dz, \qquad \gamma=3\,dx-dz.
Then
\alpha\wedge\beta\wedge\gamma = \begin{vmatrix} 1\amp1\amp0\\ 0\amp2\amp1\\ 3\amp0\amp-1 \end{vmatrix} dx\wedge dy\wedge dz.
Compute the determinant:
\begin{vmatrix} 1\amp1\amp0\\ 0\amp2\amp1\\ 3\amp0\amp-1 \end{vmatrix} \amp= 1(2(-1)-1\cdot0) - 1(0(-1)-1\cdot3) \amp= -2-(-3) \amp= 1.
So
\boxed{ \alpha\wedge\beta\wedge\gamma = dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. }

subsection.p: The wedge product has now given us the basic measuring objects. But forms in real integrals do not usually have constant coefficients. They have functions attached to them, and we need to add them, scale them, and multiply them without losing track of degree. That is the algebra of forms.
